cards.front: What are the main stages of a UIViewController lifecycle?
cards.notes: viewDidLoad is called once; appear/disappear methods are called every time.

cards.back: A constraint-based layout system that dynamically calculates the size and position of views.
cards.notes: Uses NSLayoutConstraint or visual format language.

cards.front: What is the difference between frame and bounds?
cards.back: frame is position/size relative to the superview; bounds is position/size relative to the view's own coordinate system.
cards.notes: frame.origin changes when the view moves; bounds.origin changes when content scrolls.

cards.front: What is a UITableViewCell reuse mechanism?
cards.back: Cells scrolled off-screen are placed in a reuse queue and recycled for new rows, improving performance.

cards.front: What is the difference between push and present navigation?
cards.back: Push adds to a UINavigationController stack (horizontal slide). Present shows a modal (vertical slide).
cards.notes: Push requires a navigation controller; present works on any view controller.

cards.back: A design pattern where one object delegates certain behaviors to another object via a protocol.
A hierarchy of objects (views → view controllers → window → app) that can handle events passed along the chain.
cards.notes: If a responder can't handle an event, it forwards to the next responder.

cards.back: Marks the view's layout as needing an update, which will happen on the next layout cycle.
cards.notes: Call layoutIfNeeded() to force immediate layout.

cards.back: A streamlined interface for laying out views in a row or column, managing constraints automatically.
cards.notes: axis, distribution, alignment, spacing are key properties.

cards.front: What is the difference between intrinsicContentSize and sizeThatFits?
cards.back: intrinsicContentSize is the natural size a view prefers; sizeThatFits returns the best size for a given bounding rect.
cards.notes: UILabel's intrinsicContentSize is based on its text and font.
